original_abstract: Abstract. This study investigates the photochemical aging of wood-burning aerosol produced under smoldering conditions, utilizing a 104 m³ dual outdoor chamber under natural sunlight across varying relative humidity (RH) and NOx levels. We monitored the light absorption of organic carbon (OC) throughout the photooxidation process using a UV–visible spectrometer equipped with an integrating sphere. Our results indicate that high RH leads to rapid color decay, while the presence of NOx slightly extends the lifetime of the aerosol's color, suggesting that NOx facilitates chromophore formation through secondary pathways. Generally, the mass absorption cross-section of OC (integrated from 280 to 600 nm) rose by 11–54% during the morning hours—with the exception of high RH conditions—before gradually declining by 19–68% in the afternoon. This dynamic evolution in light absorption is driven by the competing mechanisms of chromophore formation and sunlight-induced bleaching. To further resolve the relationship between chemical transformation and optical properties, we characterized the particles using multiple spectroscopic techniques. A rapid decrease in fluorescence intensity pointed toward the bleaching of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), while falling levoglucosan concentrations confirmed the steady depletion of primary organic aerosol. Additionally, infrared spectroscopy revealed that the organic particles became less hygroscopic over time. These experimental trends mirror observations of ambient smoke from the 2012 County Line wildfire in Florida. Ultimately, we conclude that biomass-burning OC undergoes a net reduction in light absorption after approximately 8–9 hours of sunlight exposure compared to its initial, fresh state.
humanized_abstract: Irradiation of smoldering biomass smoke in a large outdoor photoreactor Photochemical Aging of Smoldering Biomass Smoke Aging of Wood Smoke in a Dual Outdoor Photoreactor In-situ studies of photochemical aging of biomass smoke under realistic atmospheric conditions provide a crucial link between biomass burning emissions and their subsequent effects on climate forcing. Here we investigate the photochemical aging of biomass smoke from smoldering wood burns using a 104 m 3 dual outdoor chamber under solar irradiation over a wide range of relative humidity (RH) and NO x conditions. Bio-mass smoke color evolution and resulting light absorption of organic carbon (OC) measured using a UV-Vis spectrometer equipped with an integrating sphere was measured as a function of time and conditions. In-situ measurements reveal that the high RH leads to faster color decay of the aerosol, while small enhancements in aerosol color stability by up to a factor of 1.05 is observed at high NO x levels. In general, the mass absorption cross-section (MAC) of OC (integrated over 280–600 nm) was found to increase by 11–54% during the morning photochemical processes (except for the case of high RH) and then decrease by 19–68% in the afternoon. We carried out multi-spectroscopic studies to probe in detail the detailed evolution of biomass smoke photochemistry, elucidating the relationship between the chemistry and aerosol optical properties. Rapid photochemical processes resulted in fast bleach-out of PAH fluorescence signal and declining levoglucosan concentrations, confirming the ongoing depletion of primary organic aerosol (OA). Moreover, the aerosol became less hygroscopic over time based on the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. Similar trends were observed from the analysis of field data from ambient smoke from the 2012 County Line wildfire in Florida. Thus, biomass-burning OC leads to a net decrease in light absorption after around 8–9 hours of photochemical aging under sunlight conditions.
